1	\chapter{Section graded ring infrastructure: tensor powers and graded sections}
2	\label{chap:Picard_SectionGradedRing}
3	
4	\providecommand{\PshMod}{\mathrm{PresheafOfModules}}
5	\providecommand{\ShMod}{\mathrm{SheafOfModules}}
6	\providecommand{\Modules}{\mathrm{Modules}}
7	
8	The Hilbert-polynomial route of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} represents the
9	degree-\(m\) graded component of a fibre by the global sections
10	\(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) of a twist of a coherent
11	sheaf by a tensor power of a line bundle, and it organises these components into
12	a graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
13	(\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) and a graded module
14	\(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0}
15	\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
16	(\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}). Three pieces of that picture have no direct
17	Mathlib counterpart and must be built here, each in its own section:
18	
19	\begin{enumerate}
20	  \item \textbf{Tensor powers of a sheaf of modules}
21	    (\cref{sec:sgr_tensor_powers}). Mathlib equips the category
22	    \(\PshMod\) of \emph{presheaves} of modules over a presheaf of commutative
23	    rings with a symmetric monoidal structure
24	    (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) and provides the
25	    sheafification functor \(\PshMod \to \ShMod\)
26	    (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}); but the category
27	    \(\ShMod\) of \emph{sheaves} of modules carries \emph{no} monoidal
28	    structure. The tensor product of sheaves of modules
29	    \(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\) must therefore be built
30	    as the sheafification of the pointwise tensor presheaf, and the iterated
31	    tensor power \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}\) recursively from it.
32	  \item \textbf{Lax-monoidal global sections} (\cref{sec:sgr_lax_sections}).
33	    The global-sections functor \(\Gamma\) is only \emph{lax} monoidal: from the
34	    pointwise-strict monoidality of the presheaf tensor product and the
35	    sheafification unit one obtains a natural multiplication
36	    \(\Gamma(\mathcal{F}) \otimes_{\Gamma(\mathcal{O}_X)} \Gamma(\mathcal{G})
37	    \to \Gamma(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})\), together with
38	    the associativity and unit coherence those products must satisfy.
39	  \item \textbf{Graded-ring / graded-module assembly}
40	    (\cref{sec:sgr_graded_assembly}). The section multiplications and the
41	    tensor-power comparison isomorphisms assemble, through Mathlib's
42	    external-direct-sum graded API (\cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib},
43	    \cref{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib}), into the graded \emph{ring} structure
44	    on \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) and the graded
45	    \emph{module} structure on
46	    \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\).
47	\end{enumerate}
48	
49	\section{Tensor powers of a sheaf of modules}
50	\label{sec:sgr_tensor_powers}
51	
52	We work over a fixed scheme \(X\) (in the application \(X = X_s\), a fibre over a
53	field \(\kappa(s)\)). Its category of sheaves of modules is
54	\(X.\Modules = \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\), with global-sections notation
55	\(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{M}) = \mathcal{M}(X)\) for the value of the underlying
56	presheaf on the top open. The structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) is a sheaf of
57	commutative rings, so its underlying presheaf factors through the category of
58	commutative rings; this is the hypothesis under which Mathlib's presheaf-level
59	monoidal structure applies.
60	
61	\begin{lemma}[Monoidal structure on presheaves of modules]
62	  \label{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
63	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}
64	  \mathlibok
65	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
66	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Monoidal}).}
67	  Let \(R : \mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{CommRingCat}\) be a presheaf of
68	  commutative rings. Then the category \(\PshMod(R)\) of presheaves of modules
69	  over \(R\) carries a symmetric monoidal structure whose tensor product is
70	  computed objectwise: for presheaves of modules \(\mathcal{M}_1, \mathcal{M}_2\)
71	  and an object \(U\),
72	  \[
73	    (\mathcal{M}_1 \otimes \mathcal{M}_2)(U)
74	      \;=\; \mathcal{M}_1(U) \otimes_{R(U)} \mathcal{M}_2(U),
75	  \]
76	  with restriction maps the tensor products of the restriction maps. The unit is
77	  the presheaf \(R\) viewed as a module over itself, and the associator,
78	  unitors, and braiding are induced objectwise from those of
79	  \(\mathrm{ModuleCat}\). In particular evaluation at a fixed object \(U\) is a
80	  \emph{strict} monoidal functor
81	  \(\PshMod(R) \to \mathrm{ModuleCat}(R(U))\).
82	\end{lemma}
83	
84	\begin{lemma}[Sheafification of presheaves of modules]
85	  \label{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
86	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.sheafification}
87	  \mathlibok
88	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
89	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Sheafification}).}
90	  Let \(R\) be a sheaf of (commutative) rings on a site, with underlying
91	  presheaf \(R_0\). There is a sheafification functor
92	  \[
93	    (-)^{\#} \;:\; \PshMod(R_0) \longrightarrow \ShMod(R),
94	  \]
95	  left adjoint to the forgetful functor, together with the unit
96	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{M}_0} : \mathcal{M}_0 \to (\mathcal{M}_0)^{\#}\) of the
97	  adjunction. On underlying sheaves of abelian groups it agrees with the
98	  ordinary sheafification, and it sends a presheaf of \(R_0\)-modules to the
99	  associated sheaf of \(R\)-modules.
100	\end{lemma}
101	
102	\begin{lemma}[The structure sheaf as the unit module]
103	  \label{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
104	  \lean{SheafOfModules.unit}
105	  \mathlibok
106	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
107	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf}).}
108	  For a sheaf of rings \(R\) (in the application \(R = \mathcal{O}_X\)) the
109	  structure sheaf, viewed as a module over itself, is a distinguished object
110	  \(\mathbf{1} = \mathcal{O}_X \in \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\). It is the value at
111	  the zeroth tensor power, \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathcal{O}_X\), and its
112	  global sections \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) form the degree-zero component of
113	  the section graded ring.
114	\end{lemma}
115	
116	\begin{definition}\leanok
117	[Presheaf-of-modules category in monoidal form]
118	  \label{def:monoidalPresheaf}
119	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.MonoidalPresheaf}
120	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
121	  For a scheme \(X\), write \(\PshMod_{\otimes}(X)\) for the category of presheaves
122	  of modules over the underlying presheaf of (plain) rings of \(\mathcal{O}_X\),
123	  obtained by composing the structure sheaf with the forgetful functor
124	  \(\mathrm{CommRingCat} \to \mathrm{RingCat}\). This is definitionally
125	  \(X.\PshMod\), but presented in the exact form for which Mathlib equips it with
126	  the symmetric monoidal structure of \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib};
127	  it is the carrier on which the objectwise tensor product of
128	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} is taken.
129	\end{definition}
130	
131	\begin{definition}\leanok
132	[Scheme-level module sheafification functor]
133	  \label{def:schemeModuleSheafification}
134	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sheafification}
135	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
136	  For a scheme \(X\), the \emph{module sheafification functor}
137	  \((-)^{\#} : X.\PshMod \to X.\Modules\) is Mathlib's sheafification functor
138	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}) specialised to the identity
139	  morphism of the underlying presheaf of rings of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) (a locally
140	  bijective map). Applying it to the objectwise tensor presheaf is what defines the
141	  sheaf tensor product \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}.
142	\end{definition}
143	
144	\begin{definition}\leanok
145	[Unit module of a scheme]
146	  \label{def:unitModule}
147	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.unitModule}
148	  \uses{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
149	  For a scheme \(X\), the \emph{unit module} \(\mathbf{1}_X \in X.\Modules\) is the
150	  structure sheaf viewed as a module over itself, i.e.\ Mathlib's
151	  \(\mathrm{SheafOfModules.unit}\) (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}) for
152	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\). It is the value of the zeroth tensor power,
153	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathbf{1}_X\) (\cref{def:sheafTensorPow}).
154	\end{definition}
155	
156	\begin{definition}\leanok
157	[Tensor product of sheaves of modules]
158	  \label{def:sheafTensorObj}
159	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
160	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
161	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
162	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Tensor product" (Tag
163	  % 01CA), prose definition (read from references/stacks-modules.tex,
164	  % L2282-L2297).
165	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
166	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space and let
167	  %  $\mathcal{F}$ and $\mathcal{G}$ be $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
168	  %  We define first the {\it tensor product presheaf}
169	  %  $$
170	  %  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{p, \mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
171	  %  $$
172	  %  as the rule which assigns to $U \subset X$ open the
173	  %  $\mathcal{O}_X(U)$-module
174	  %  $\mathcal{F}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{G}(U)$.
175	  %  Having defined this we define the {\it tensor product sheaf}
176	  %  as the sheafification of the above:
177	  %  $$
178	  %  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
179	  %  =
180	  %  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p, \mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})^\#
181	  %  $$"
182	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CA (tensor
183	  product).}
184	  Let \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}\) be sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Their
185	  \emph{tensor product} is the sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
186	  \[
187	    \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
188	      \;=\; \bigl(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\bigr)^{\#},
189	  \]
190	  the sheafification (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}) of the
191	  tensor product presheaf \(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\),
192	  whose value on an open \(U\) is the \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module
193	  \(\mathcal{F}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{G}(U)\). Concretely, the
194	  tensor product presheaf is the Mathlib presheaf-level monoidal product
195	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) of the underlying presheaves of
196	  modules of \(\mathcal{F}\) and \(\mathcal{G}\), and
197	  \(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\) is its sheafification. This
198	  construction is symmetric and associative because the presheaf tensor product
199	  is, and sheafification preserves the coherence isomorphisms.
200	\end{definition}
201	
202	\begin{definition}\leanok
203	[Tensor power of a line bundle]
204	  \label{def:sheafTensorPow}
205	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPow}
206	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, def:unitModule, lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
207	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules",
208	  % `definition-powers' (Tag 01CU) (read from references/stacks-modules.tex,
209	  % L4220-L4228).
210	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
211	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. Given an invertible sheaf
212	  %  $\mathcal{L}$ on $X$ and $n \in \mathbf{Z}$ we define the
213	  %  $n$th {\it tensor power} $\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n}$ of $\mathcal{L}$
214	  %  as the image of $\mathcal{O}_X$ under applying the equivalence
215	  %  $\mathcal{F} \mapsto\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}$
216	  %  exactly $n$ times."
217	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CU (tensor
218	  powers).}
219	  Let \(\mathcal{L}\) be a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (in the application
220	  an invertible sheaf / line bundle \(L_s\)). For \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) the
221	  \emph{\(m\)th tensor power} \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}\) is defined by recursion
222	  on \(m\):
223	  \[
224	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathcal{O}_X
225	      \quad(\text{the unit module, \cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}}),
226	    \qquad
227	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)}
228	      = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}
229	        \quad(\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}).
230	  \]
231	  We restrict to non-negative powers, which is all the section graded ring uses;
232	  the Stacks definition extends \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n}\) to all
233	  \(n \in \mathbf{Z}\) using invertibility, but negative powers are not needed
234	  here. For \(\mathcal{L}\) invertible each \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}\) is again
235	  invertible.
236	\end{definition}
237	
238	\begin{lemma}\leanok
239	[Zeroth tensor power is the unit]
240	  \label{lem:tensorPow_zero}
241	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPow_zero}
242	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:unitModule}
243	  For a sheaf of modules \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(X\) one has
244	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathbf{1}_X\), the unit module
245	  (\cref{def:unitModule}); this is the base clause of the recursive definition
246	  \cref{def:sheafTensorPow}, holding by definition.
247	\end{lemma}
248	
249	\begin{lemma}\leanok
250	[Tensor-power recursion step]
251	  \label{lem:tensorPow_succ}
252	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPow_succ}
253	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:sheafTensorObj}
254	  For a sheaf of modules \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(X\) and \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) one has
255	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)} = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
256	  \mathcal{L}\) (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}); this is the successor clause of
257	  \cref{def:sheafTensorPow}, holding by definition.
258	\end{lemma}
259	
260	\begin{definition}\leanok
261	[Twist of a coherent sheaf by a tensor power]
262	  \label{def:sheafModuleTwist}
263	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.moduleTensorPow}
264	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, def:sheafTensorPow}
265	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (in the application
266	  a coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}_s\)) and \(\mathcal{L}\) a line bundle. For
267	  \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) the \emph{\(m\)-twist} of \(\mathcal{F}\) by
268	  \(\mathcal{L}\) is
269	  \[
270	    \mathcal{F}(m) \;:=\;
271	      \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}
272	      \qquad(\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}, \cref{def:sheafTensorPow}),
273	  \]
274	  the degree-\(m\) carrier of the section graded module. By construction
275	  \(\mathcal{F}(0) = \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{O}_X \cong
276	  \mathcal{F}\).
277	\end{definition}
278	
279	\begin{lemma}\leanok
280	[Zeroth twist]
281	  \label{lem:moduleTensorPow_zero}
282	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.moduleTensorPow_zero}
283	  \uses{def:sheafModuleTwist, def:sheafTensorObj, def:unitModule,
284	    def:tensorObjRightUnitor}
285	  For sheaves of modules \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{L}\) on \(X\) one has
286	  \(\mathcal{F}(0) = \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathbf{1}_X\)
287	  (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}, \cref{def:unitModule}); this is the base clause of
288	  \cref{def:sheafModuleTwist}, holding by definition, and is canonically
289	  isomorphic to \(\mathcal{F}\) via the right unitor
290	  (\cref{def:tensorObjRightUnitor}).
291	\end{lemma}
292	
293	The next four isomorphisms are the parts of the (would-be) symmetric monoidal
294	structure on \(X.\Modules\) that descend through sheafification from
295	\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib} using only \emph{functoriality} of the
296	sheafification functor (\cref{def:schemeModuleSheafification}) and, for the
297	unitors, the reflective counit isomorphism --- no strong monoidality of
298	sheafification is required. They are the structural inputs of the tensor-power
299	comparison \cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}.

301	\begin{definition}\leanok
302	[Counit isomorphism of module sheafification]
303	  \label{def:sheafificationCounitIso}
304	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sheafificationCounitIso}
305	  \uses{def:schemeModuleSheafification, lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
306	  For a sheaf of modules \(\mathcal{G}\) on \(X\) the counit of the sheafification
307	  adjunction (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib},
308	  \cref{def:schemeModuleSheafification}) gives a canonical isomorphism
309	  \((\mathcal{G})^{\#} \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{G}\) of sheaves of modules,

311	  is fully faithful.
312	\end{definition}
313	
314	\begin{definition}\leanok
315	[Left unitor of the sheaf tensor product]
316	  \label{def:tensorObjUnitIso}
317	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjUnitIso}
318	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, def:unitModule, def:sheafificationCounitIso,
319	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}

321	  isomorphism \(\mathbf{1}_X \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
322	  \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{G}\) (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj},
323	  \cref{def:unitModule}), obtained by sheafifying the presheaf left unitor of

331	  \label{def:tensorObjRightUnitor}
332	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjRightUnitor}
333	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, def:unitModule, def:sheafificationCounitIso,
334	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
335	  For a sheaf of modules \(\mathcal{G}\) on \(X\) there is a canonical right-unitor

342	
343	\begin{definition}\leanok
344	[Braiding of the sheaf tensor product]
345	  \label{def:tensorBraiding}
346	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorBraiding}
347	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, def:schemeModuleSheafification,
348	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}

358	The strong-monoidality comparison \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
359	below is assembled from three reduction lemmas --- a localization criterion, the
360	local-isomorphism property of the unit, and their corollary --- together with the
361	coequalizer presentation of the relative module-presheaf tensor product, the single
362	Mathlib-absent brick on which the comparison rests.

364	\begin{lemma}\leanok

443	\begin{lemma}[A \(\mathbb{Z}\)-whiskered stalkwise isomorphism is a local isomorphism]
444	  \label{lem:snap_ztensor_whisker_localIso}
445	  \uses{lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit, def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}
446	  Let \(f : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be a morphism of abelian-group
447	  presheaves on the space \(X\) that is a \emph{stalkwise isomorphism} (each
448	  stalk map \(f_x\) is an isomorphism), and let \(\mathcal{C}\) be a further
449	  abelian-group presheaf. Then the whiskered map
450	  \(f \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}_{\mathcal{C}}\) of \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor
451	  presheaves is again a stalkwise isomorphism, hence a local isomorphism (a
452	  member of \(J.W\) for the opens topology \(J\)).
453	\end{lemma}
454	
455	\begin{proof}
456	  \uses{def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}
457	  The stalk functor \(\mathcal{A} \mapsto \mathcal{A}_x\) at a point \(x\) is a
458	  filtered colimit over the neighbourhoods of \(x\), and \(\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}\)
459	  commutes with filtered colimits, so
460	  \((\mathcal{A} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{C})_x \cong
461	  \mathcal{A}_x \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{C}_x\) naturally in \(\mathcal{A}\).
462	  Under this identification the stalk of \(f \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\) is
463	  \(f_x \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\), an isomorphism since \(f_x\) is.
464	  Thus \(f \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\) is a stalkwise isomorphism; a
465	  stalkwise isomorphism of abelian-group presheaves on a space is exactly a local
466	  isomorphism, i.e.\ lies in \(J.W\).
467	\end{proof}

470	  \label{lem:tensorProduct_liftAddHom_mathlib}
471	  \lean{TensorProduct.liftAddHom}

473	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.TensorProduct.Basic}).}
474	  Let \(S\) be a commutative ring and \(M, N, P\) be \(S\)-modules (or, more
475	  generally, the abelian-group statement: \(M, N\) modules and \(P\) an additive
476	  commutative group). An additive-bilinear map
477	  \(f : M \to (N \to P)\) that is \emph{\(S\)-balanced}, i.e.\ satisfies
478	  \(f(s \cdot m)(n) = f(m)(s \cdot n)\) for all \(s \in S\), factors uniquely
479	  through the relative tensor product as an additive group homomorphism
480	  \(\widetilde{f} : M \otimes_S N \to P\) with
481	  \(\widetilde{f}(m \otimes_S n) = f(m)(n)\). This is the abelian-group universal
482	  property of \(M \otimes_S N\): it is the quotient of \(M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N\)
483	  by the \(S\)-balancing relations.

486	\begin{lemma}[The relative tensor of modules is a coequalizer of abelian groups]
487	  \label{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer}
488	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.isColimitCofork,
489	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actN,
490	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actM,
491	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actLmap,
492	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actRmap,
493	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.projL,
494	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.projL_surjective,
495	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.projL_comp_act,
496	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.aL,
497	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.aR,
498	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.piMor,
499	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.piMor_epi,
500	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.coeq_condition,
501	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.cofork,
502	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.descHom,
503	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.descMor,
504	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.descFac,
505	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actLmap_tmul,
506	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actRmap_tmul,
507	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.projL_tmul,
508	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.piMor_apply,
509	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.descHom_tmul}
510	  \uses{lem:tensorProduct_liftAddHom_mathlib}
511	  Let \(S\) be a commutative ring and \(M, N\) be \(S\)-modules. The abelian group
512	  \(M \otimes_S N\) is the coequalizer, \emph{in the category
513	  \(\mathrm{AddCommGrp}\) of abelian groups}, of the two parallel
514	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-linear \(S\)-action maps
515	  \[
516	    a_L,\, a_R :
517	    M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} (S \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N)
518	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
519	    M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N,
520	  \]
521	  where the \emph{left} action map sends \(m \otimes (s \otimes n) \mapsto
522	  (s \cdot m) \otimes n\) and the \emph{right} action map sends
523	  \(m \otimes (s \otimes n) \mapsto m \otimes (s \cdot n)\). The coforking map is
524	  the canonical projection \(\pi : M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N \to M \otimes_S N\),
525	  \(m \otimes n \mapsto m \otimes_S n\).
526	\end{lemma}
527	
528	\begin{proof}
529	  \uses{lem:tensorProduct_liftAddHom_mathlib}
530	  Write \(a_N : S \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N \to N\) and
531	  \(a_M : M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} S \to M\) for the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-linear maps
532	  induced by the scalar action (\(s \otimes n \mapsto s \cdot n\),
533	  \(m \otimes s \mapsto s \cdot m\)); the right action map is
534	  \(a_R = \mathrm{id}_M \otimes a_N\) and the left action map is
535	  \(a_L = (a_M \otimes \mathrm{id}_N) \circ \mathrm{assoc}^{-1}\), where
536	  \(\mathrm{assoc}\) is the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor associator. The projection
537	  \(\pi\) is the additive homomorphism produced by the abelian universal property
538	  \cref{lem:tensorProduct_liftAddHom_mathlib} from the \(S\)-balanced bilinear map
539	  \((m, n) \mapsto m \otimes_S n\).
540	
541	  \emph{Cofork condition.} On an elementary tensor
542	  \(m \otimes (s \otimes n)\) we have
543	  \(\pi(a_L(m \otimes s \otimes n)) = (s \cdot m) \otimes_S n
544	  = m \otimes_S (s \cdot n) = \pi(a_R(m \otimes s \otimes n))\), the middle
545	  equality being the defining \(S\)-balancing relation of \(M \otimes_S N\); since
546	  elementary tensors generate, \(\pi \circ a_L = \pi \circ a_R\).
547	
548	  \emph{Universal property.} Given any abelian group \(P\) and a homomorphism
549	  \(g : M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N \to P\) with \(g \circ a_L = g \circ a_R\), the
550	  bilinear map \((m, n) \mapsto g(m \otimes n)\) is \(S\)-balanced (that is exactly
551	  the cofork condition read on elementary tensors), so by
552	  \cref{lem:tensorProduct_liftAddHom_mathlib} it factors as a unique
553	  \(\widetilde{g} : M \otimes_S N \to P\) with \(\widetilde{g} \circ \pi = g\).
554	  Existence is this descended map; uniqueness follows because \(\pi\) is
555	  surjective, hence an epimorphism of abelian groups, so \(\widetilde{g} \circ \pi
556	  = g\) determines \(\widetilde{g}\). Packaging existence and uniqueness gives the
557	  colimit cofork.
558	
559	\end{proof}

561	\begin{lemma}[Relative tensor of module presheaves as a coequalizer]
562	  \label{lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer}
563	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.relativeTensorCoequalizerIso}
564	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
565	    lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer,
566	    lem:evaluationJointlyReflectsColimits_mathlib,
567	    lem:presheaf_tensorObj_obj_mathlib}
568	  Let \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) be presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The
569	  underlying abelian-group presheaf of their relative tensor product
570	  \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{Q}\)
571	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) is naturally the coequalizer of the
572	  parallel pair
573	  \[
574	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
575	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
576	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
577	  \]
578	  of abelian-group presheaves, where the first map acts the ring factor on the left,
579	  \(p \otimes r \otimes q \mapsto (r \cdot p) \otimes q\), and the second on the
580	  right, \(p \otimes r \otimes q \mapsto p \otimes (r \cdot q)\). The identification
581	  is objectwise and natural in \(U\).
582	\end{lemma}
583	
584	\begin{proof}
585	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
586	    lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer,
587	    lem:evaluationJointlyReflectsColimits_mathlib,
588	    lem:presheaf_tensorObj_obj_mathlib}
589	  The argument promotes the objectwise coequalizer of
590	  \cref{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer} to the functor category, in
591	  three steps.
592	
593	  \textbf{1. Objectwise.} For each open \(U\), apply
594	  \cref{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer} with \(S = \mathcal{O}_X(U)\),
595	  \(M = \mathcal{P}(U)\), \(N = \mathcal{Q}(U)\): the abelian group
596	  \(\mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U)\) is the coequalizer
597	  in \(\mathrm{AddCommGrp}\) of the two \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-action maps
598	  \[
599	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X(U)
600	      \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)
601	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
602	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U),
603	  \]
604	  with cofork map the projection \(\pi_U\).
605	
606	  \textbf{2. Naturality / promotion.} The three objectwise families assemble into
607	  morphisms of abelian-group presheaves
608	  \[
609	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X
610	      \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
611	    \;\overset{a_L,\,a_R}{\rightrightarrows}\;
612	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
613	    \;\overset{\pi}{\longrightarrow}\;
614	    \bigl(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U)\bigr),
615	  \]
616	  i.e.\ natural transformations of functors
617	  \(\mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{AddCommGrp}\). Naturality of
618	  \(a_L, a_R, \pi\) in \(U\) is the compatibility of the module action with the
619	  restriction maps of \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{O}_X, \mathcal{Q}\): the restriction
620	  of \(s \cdot m\) is \((s|_V) \cdot (m|_V)\), and likewise for the projection,
621	  because the relative tensor is functorial in \(U\). Colimits in a functor
622	  category are computed objectwise --- by
623	  \cref{lem:evaluationJointlyReflectsColimits_mathlib} a cocone is a colimit as
624	  soon as every evaluation \((\mathrm{ev}_U)\) of it is --- so the objectwise
625	  coequalizers of step 1 assemble into a coequalizer of the displayed parallel
626	  pair in \(\mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{AddCommGrp}\).
627	
628	  \textbf{3. Identification of the apex.} The apex presheaf
629	  \(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U)\) is, by the
630	  objectwise tensor formula of the presheaf monoidal structure
631	  (\cref{lem:presheaf_tensorObj_obj_mathlib}: \((\mathcal{M}_1 \otimes_p
632	  \mathcal{M}_2)(U) = \mathcal{M}_1(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}
633	  \mathcal{M}_2(U)\)), exactly the underlying abelian-group presheaf of the
634	  relative tensor product \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{Q}\)
635	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}). Transporting the colimit of step 2
636	  along this identification exhibits
637	  \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{Q}\), as an abelian-group
638	  presheaf, as the coequalizer of the parallel pair, naturally in \(U\).
639	\end{proof}

674	\begin{lemma}[Sheafification inverts the whiskered localization unit]
675	  \label{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
676	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
677	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
678	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib,
679	    lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff, lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit,
680	    lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer,
681	    lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer}
682	  Let \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) be presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules,
683	  let \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} : \mathcal{P} \to (\mathcal{P})^{\#}\) be the unit of
684	  the sheafification adjunction
685	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}), viewed as a morphism of
686	  presheaves of modules, and write
687	  \[
688	    \eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q} :
689	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}
690	    \longrightarrow (\mathcal{P})^{\#} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}
691	  \]
692	  for its right whiskering by \(\mathcal{Q}\) in the presheaf monoidal structure
693	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}). Then the image of
694	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) under the sheafification functor,
695	  \[
696	    (\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q})^{\#} :
697	    (\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q})^{\#}
698	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
699	    \bigl((\mathcal{P})^{\#} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\bigr)^{\#},
700	  \]
701	  is an isomorphism of sheaves of modules. This is the strong-monoidality
702	  comparison of the module sheafification functor on a whiskered unit.
703	\end{lemma}
704	
705	\begin{proof}
706	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
707	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib,
708	    lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff, lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit,
709	    lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer,
710	    lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer,
711	    lem:snap_ztensor_whisker_localIso, def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}
712	  Module sheafification is a localization functor: it inverts exactly the class
713	  \(W\) of morphisms of presheaves of modules whose underlying morphism of
714	  abelian-group presheaves is a local isomorphism --- that is, lies in the class
715	  \(J.W\) of weak equivalences attached to the Grothendieck topology \(J\) on
716	  \(X\) (the inverse image of \(J.W\) along the forgetful functor to
717	  abelian-group presheaves). By the localization criterion for a reflective
718	  adjunction, \((f)^{\#}\) is an isomorphism if and only if \(f \in W\), i.e.\
719	  if and only if the underlying abelian-presheaf morphism of \(f\) lies in
720	  \(J.W\). It therefore suffices to prove that the underlying abelian morphism
721	  of \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) lies in \(J.W\).
722	
723	  \textbf{The relative tensor as a coequalizer.} The underlying abelian-group
724	  presheaf of the relative tensor product \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\)
725	  is the objectwise relative tensor
726	  \(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U)\). For each
727	  \(U\) this module is the coequalizer of the two \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-action
728	  maps
729	  \[
730	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X(U)
731	      \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)
732	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
733	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U),
734	  \]
735	  one acting on the left and one on the right factor. Colimits of abelian-group
736	  presheaves are computed objectwise, so \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\),
737	  as an abelian-group presheaf, is the coequalizer of the corresponding parallel
738	  pair
739	  \[
740	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X
741	      \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
742	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
743	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
744	  \]
745	  of \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaves. Under this presentation the underlying
746	  abelian morphism of \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) is the map of
747	  coequalizers induced by whiskering the two rows with
748	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} (-)\).
749	
750	  \textbf{Abelian sheafification preserves the coequalizer.} The abelian
751	  sheafification functor \(a\) (the associated-sheaf functor for \(J\) on
752	  abelian-group presheaves) is a left adjoint, hence preserves coequalizers; it
753	  therefore carries the parallel pair and its induced map to the analogous data
754	  of \(a\)-images. It remains to see that the two whiskered comparison maps
755	  relating the rows,
756	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\) and
757	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}
758	  \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\), lie in \(J.W\) (equivalently, become

760	
761	  \textbf{Whiskered units are stalkwise isomorphisms.} We argue this via stalks
762	  of abelian-group presheaves (\(\mathrm{TopCat.Presheaf.stalk}\), which exist

63	
64	# Blueprint Writer
65	
66	You write or revise **one blueprint chapter** under plan-agent direction. You receive a precise directive naming the target chapter, the strategy context the chapter must reflect, the definitions/theorems that must be present, and the scope of changes you may make.
67	
68	## Your Job
69	
70	The plan agent has decided that a specific blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`) needs to change — to reflect a strategic decision, fill a missing definition or theorem the project needs, or align the informal prose with the current Lean structure. Your directive tells you which chapter, what must be there, and what counts as out-of-scope.
71	
72	You only edit the **one chapter named in your directive**. Your declared `--write-domain` should reflect that (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`). You do NOT edit other chapters, `.lean` files, or `content.tex`. Cross-chapter inconsistencies you spot go in the report's "Notes for Plan Agent" section — you flag them, you don't fix them.
73	
74	## Directive Format
75	
76	```markdown
77	# Blueprint Writer Directive
78	
79	## Slug
80	<slug>
81	
82	## Target chapter
83	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex
84	
85	## Strategy context
86	<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted for you — typically a paragraph or two describing what this chapter must support in the overall arc. You do NOT read STRATEGY.md yourself; the plan agent gives you only what is relevant.>
87	
88	## Required content
89	- Definition <name>: <informal description of what it must define, with the mathematical content the prover needs in order to formalize>
90	- Theorem <name>: <statement + intent of the proof sketch the prover should formalize>
91	- Proof sketch for <theorem>: <how detailed; what cross-references; whether to expand a step into sub-lemmas>
92	- ...
93	
94	## Out of scope
95	<things that look related but the plan agent does NOT want you to touch this round>
96	
97	## References
98	- <path/to/reference.md or arxiv ID>: <which sections are relevant>
99	- ...
100	
101	## Expected outcome
102	<what the chapter should look like after, in one paragraph>
103	```
104	
105	If a section is omitted from your directive, the plan agent decided you don't need it. Don't speculate beyond what was given.
106	
107	## Chapter format
108	
109	Each declaration block in a chapter looks like:
110	
111	```latex
112	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
113	  \label{thm:some_label}
114	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
115	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
116	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174  (read from references/hartshorne-III-5.md)
117	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A morphism $f: X \to Y$ of schemes locally of finite
118	  % type is said to be smooth at $x \in X$ if there exist an open affine
119	  % neighborhood $V = \Spec B$ of $f(x)$ and an open affine neighborhood
120	  % $U = \Spec A$ of $x$ with $f(U) \subset V$ such that ..."
121	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.5.1.}
122	  Informal statement of the theorem, in the project's notation.
123	\end{theorem}
124	
125	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. We may assume $Y = \Spec B$ and
126	% $X = \Spec A$ are affine. Then $f$ corresponds to a ring homomorphism
127	% $\varphi: B \to A$, and $f$ is smooth at $x$ if and only if ..."
128	\begin{proof}
129	  \uses{thm:another_result}
130	  Step-by-step informal proof, in the project's notation. Reference blueprint labels
131	  with \uses{...} so the dependency graph stays accurate.
132	\end{proof}
133	```
134	
135	Use `\definition`, `\lemma`, `\theorem`, `\proposition`, `\corollary` as appropriate. `\lean{...}` names the Lean declaration this block corresponds to. `\uses{...}` records cross-references; keep it accurate so `leanblueprint`'s dependency graph remains usable.
136	
137	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
138	
139	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
140	
141	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
142	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
143	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
144	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
145	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
146	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
147	
148	**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). Same verbatim rules — original language, original notation, every word. The informal proof body inside `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.
149	
150	When a source proof is too long for one verbatim block (multi-page construction): split the theorem into sub-lemmas in the directive's logical structure, and give each sub-statement its own `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`. One opaque mega-quote defeats verifiability. If even sub-splitting is impractical, report it in "Notes for Plan Agent" — do not silently drop the verbatim quote.
151	
152	**For Archon-original / project-bespoke results** (the directive does not name an external source for this block): the source lines are omitted — the block stands on the proof sketch alone.
153	
154	**The hard rule, explicit:**
155	
156	> **You may NEVER write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from training memory.** Every such line must be backed by a local file under `references/` that you have **opened and read in this session**, and the verbatim quote must be a character-by-character copy of text from that file.
157	
158	The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical in `% SOURCE:` is your discipline check. If, when reviewing your own draft, you find a block where you cannot point to the specific local file you read to produce the quote — you fabricated it. Delete the quote. Either dispatch a `reference-retriever` (see below) to obtain the missing source, or report the block as INCOMPLETE under "Notes for Plan Agent".
159	
160	If the directive named a source but the local reference file is missing or doesn't contain the specific statement you need:
161	
162	- **Dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** (see "Dispatching a reference-retriever" below) to fetch the missing material into `references/<slug>.md`.
163	- **Wait for the retriever to return**, then **open and read the new file**.
164	- THEN write the citation block, citing the new local file in the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
165	
166	If retrieval fails (paywall, broken link, no API key, not available online): mark the block `% SOURCE: <pointer> (verbatim text not yet retrieved)` and skip it. Report it as INCOMPLETE. Do NOT substitute a paraphrase, a recollection, or a translation as the verbatim quote — that's the iter-149 failure mode and the entire rule exists to prevent it.
167	
168	## Rules
169	
170	### What you CAN do
171	- Add new declaration blocks (definitions, lemmas, theorems, propositions, corollaries) under direction.
172	- Expand or revise existing prose / proof sketches in your assigned chapter.
173	- **Add, correct, or complete `\uses{...}` cross-references** so the dependency edges match the real mathematics. If your chapter's proof of `T` invokes lemma `L`, `T` must declare `\uses{lem:L}`; if a `\uses{}` points at a label that no longer exists, fix it. Verify with `leandag` (see "Verifying dependencies with leandag" below) — do not eyeball it.
174	- Adjust `\lean{...}` hints when the directive names a new Lean target.
175	- **Write entries for Lean helper declarations.** The project keeps a 1-to-1 Lean ↔ blueprint correspondence: when the directive lists a helper that exists only in Lean (a prover-created lemma, an instance, a decomposition step), give it a real entry — concise informal statement, `\label{}`, `\lean{}` with the exact Lean name, accurate `\uses{}`, and at least a one-line informal proof. "It's trivial" is not a reason to skip it: the entry is what carries the dependency edges and what helps a later prover fill the sorry. When the helper's Lean proof relies on a fact with no blueprint entry, flag it in "Notes for Plan Agent" (or create it too, if it belongs in your chapter).
176	- **Author a *Mathlib dependency anchor* and mark it `\mathlibok`** when your chapter relies on a result Mathlib already provides — write out the statement and point `\lean{}` at the real Mathlib declaration (see "Mathlib dependency anchors" below). This is the only marker you may add.
177	- **Remove a declaration block ONLY when the directive explicitly lists it for removal** (typically an isolated/orphaned statement the reviewer flagged `remove`). Default to wiring an isolated node into the graph (add the missing `\uses{}`), not deleting it — an isolated node is usually a missing edge, not dead weight. Never remove the project goal, and never remove a block whose `\lean{}` still names a live Lean declaration.
178	- Read `references/summary.md` and any reference that is in `references/` to ground your writing in the project's sources.
179	
180	### What you MUST do
181	- **Keep the chapter valid LaTeX.** Don't leave dangling `\begin{...}` without matching `\end{...}`. Compile-checking is the plan agent's responsibility but you must not introduce syntax errors.
182	- **Stay within your chapter.** Your declared write-domain is one `*.tex` file. The Archon CLI rejects writes outside it.
183	- **Define non-standard macros in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex`** before using them — but: that file is outside your write-domain, so you DON'T touch it. If the directive requires a new macro, you note in your report "needs macro `\foo`" and leave the LaTeX using the new command name; the plan agent adds the macro before next iter's typeset.
184	- **Use mathematical, not Lean-syntactic prose.** Describe the proof in the language of mathematics — definitions, set inclusions, ring maps, universal properties — not in Lean tactic syntax. The prover formalizes your math.
185	- **Follow citation discipline** for every block derived from external reference material (see "Citation discipline (the hard rule)" above). Each such block needs `% SOURCE:` with a local-file parenthetical, `% SOURCE QUOTE:` with a verbatim original-language quote, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` before the proof env when applicable, and a visible `\textit{Source: ...}` prefix. Never cite from memory — only from a local `references/<file>.md` you opened and read in this session.
186	- **Document every change** in your report, including which `references/<file>.md` files you opened (under "References consulted").
187	
188	### What you MUST NOT do
189	- **Do NOT reference project history.** No "since iteration N," "after our failed attempt," or session narrative. The blueprint must read as a standalone mathematical document.
190	- **Do NOT include Lean implementation details.** No tactic suggestions, typeclass wiring notes, or Lean code structure hints in the prose. If a Lean note is needed, it belongs in `.lean` files (the plan agent will use `lean-scaffolder` for that).
191	- **Do NOT add `\leanok` markers.** `\leanok` is earned by a sorry-free Lean proof and set by the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase — never by you. (`\mathlibok` is different: you MAY add it, but ONLY on a Mathlib dependency anchor — see "Mathlib dependency anchors" below — never on the project's own to-be-proved declarations.)
192	- **Do NOT edit other chapters.** Even when you spot a related issue, flag it in "Notes for Plan Agent" instead of fixing it.
193	- **Do NOT edit `content.tex`** (the top-level blueprint file that `\input`s the chapters).
194	- **Do NOT edit `.lean` files** or any other state file.
195	- **Do NOT write Lean syntax** — keep the chapter mathematical, not syntactic.
196	- **Do NOT write literal `REF` placeholders.** Never produce prose like "Definition~REF" or "Sections REF–REF". Cross-reference declarations with `\cref{<label>}` using the real label (look it up in the chapter or via `leandag`); when the target genuinely has no label yet, describe it by name in prose. The blueprint-doctor flags every literal `REF` as a malformed reference.
197	- **Do NOT interleave math delimiters.** Pick `\( … \)` for a formula and close it with `\)` — never produce `$ … \( … \) … $` or `X\(, hence \)Y` inversions; the doctor's `math-delim` lint flags every such site and the rendered output shreds mid-formula.
198	- **Do NOT paste bare label ids into prose.** "Thm.~th:main" is not a reference — write `\cref{...}` for project declarations, or the source's human-readable number ("Thm.~4.8") when quoting external material.
199	- **Do NOT use undefined macros.** Every `\foo` your prose needs must exist in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex` or be `\providecommand`'d at the top of your chapter (chapter-local is fine; report a "needs macro" note for shared ones).
200	- **Do NOT touch protected blocks.** Your invocation prompt's "Protected by the mathematician" section lists `archon-protected.yaml` rules. A `statement`-protected label's declaration block (statement + `\label`/`\lean`/`\uses`) is frozen — you may still write its `\begin{proof}`; an `all`-protected label is entirely off-limits. You may freely `\uses{}` protected labels from other blocks. If your directive conflicts with a protection rule, stop and report the conflict instead of editing.
201	- **Do NOT expand scope.** Stick to what the directive listed under "Required content".
202	- **Do NOT fabricate citations.** Never write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from memory. If you don't have a local `references/<file>.md` containing the source text, dispatch a retriever and wait. The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical is the discipline check — if you cannot truthfully point to the file you read, the citation block is not allowed in the chapter.
203	- **Do NOT translate or restate the verbatim quote.** `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` contain the source's original language, original notation, every word as-is. Project-notation rewrites belong in the rendered prose body, not in the verbatim comment.
204	
205	## Mathlib dependency anchors (`\mathlibok`)
206	
207	When your chapter's proofs rely on a definition or lemma that **Mathlib already provides** (and the project will not re-prove), it is good practice — not just a bare `\uses{}` to nowhere — to make that dependency a first-class blueprint block: a **Mathlib dependency anchor**. Doing so:
208	
209	- makes the reliance explicit and readable (a mathematician sees exactly which Mathlib result the route stands on);
210	- gives `leandag` a real node, so the `\uses{}` resolves (no broken/unknown ref) and the node's effort is **done** rather than ∞;
211	- keeps it clear how much the route leans on Mathlib vs. on original work.
212	
213	Write the anchor like any statement block, with three differences:
214	
215	```latex
216	\begin{lemma}[Hensel's lemma]
217	  \label{lem:hensel_mathlib}
218	  \lean{Mathlib.RingTheory.Henselian.foo}   % the REAL Mathlib declaration
219	  \mathlibok
220	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
221	  Statement of the result in the project's notation.
222	\end{lemma}
223	```
224	
225	- **`\lean{}` names the actual Mathlib declaration** (with its real namespace), so `leandag` matches the node to Mathlib.
226	- **`\mathlibok`** marks it as supplied by Mathlib — the DAG treats it as done; no `\begin{proof}` is needed.
227	- No `% SOURCE:`/`% SOURCE QUOTE:` block — Mathlib is the source; the `\lean{}` target *is* the citation.
228	
229	**The anti-hallucination rule (mirrors citation discipline):** mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib and your stated form is faithful to it. A wrong or invented `\mathlibok` is worse than an ∞ hole — the loop will treat a non-existent result as available and skip proving a real gap. So:
230	
231	- Prefer anchors the **directive names** (the plan agent/reviewer identified them as Mathlib results).
232	- If you are not confident the exact statement is in Mathlib, do **not** mark `\mathlibok` — write the block without it (or leave the `\uses{}` for the reviewer to resolve) and flag it under "Notes for Plan Agent" so the reviewer can verify against Lean.
233	- Never put `\mathlibok` on one of the project's **own** to-be-proved declarations. That marker is for Mathlib-supplied results only; `\leanok` (project proofs) is never yours to add.
234	
235	## Reading references
236	
237	You write mathematics, not literature criticism — your prose must be grounded in authoritative sources, not your training memory. Before composing any new declaration block:
238	
239	1. **Read `references/summary.md`** to see the index of sources the project already has. Sources directly relevant to your chapter are your first stop.
240	2. **Read every reference file your directive names** under its `## References` section. The plan agent named those because the chapter needs them.
241	3. **Read anything in `references/summary.md` your directive didn't name but that is clearly relevant** to your chapter (same area, same theorem, same construction).
242	
243	Do NOT write content from your training memory when you could ground it in a reference instead. When the references say one thing and your memory says another, trust the references.
244	
245	## Dispatching a reference-retriever (when the project's sources don't have what you need)
246	
247	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.
248	
249	Conditions for dispatch:
250	
251	- The directive named a source (`Smith 2018`, an arXiv ID) that isn't in `references/` yet.
252	- The required content is in a known textbook (Hartshorne, Vakil, Stacks Project, etc.) but the project hasn't summarized the relevant chapter.
253	- You need a Mathlib-adjacent source (nLab, math overflow, Stacks Project) the directive didn't anticipate.
254	
255	Dispatch (Bash; treat as blocking and await the report; in your write-domain only if it includes `references/**`):
256	
257	```
258	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
259	  --name reference-retriever \
260	  --slug <kebab-slug-for-the-source> \
261	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<your-slug>/reference-retriever-<child-slug>-directive.md \
262	  --write-domain 'references/**'
263	```
264	
265	Write the directive file first; the directive format is documented in `.archon/subagents/reference-retriever.md`. The retriever returns when the new summary is on disk; **then resume writing**, citing the new reference.
266	
267	If your invocation's recorded write-domain does NOT include `references/**` (your `parent.write_domain` in `dispatch.jsonl`), the dispatch will be rejected. In that case, STOP writing the affected section, report the missing source in "Notes for Plan Agent", and finish the parts of the chapter you can still write. The plan agent will re-dispatch you next iter with the broader write-domain.
268	
269	## Workflow
270	
271	1. Read your directive completely.
272	2. **Read `references/summary.md`** and every reference your directive points at; also read sibling-chapter material that informs cross-references. Track which local files you actually opened — you will need their paths for the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
273	3. Read the target chapter currently on disk to see what's already there.

\begin{definition}[Step-1 objectwise \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor triple presheaf]
  \label{def:relTensorTriplePresheaf}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.relTensorTriplePresheaf}
  \uses{lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer}
  For presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) the
  assignment
  \[
    U \;\longmapsto\;
      \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}
        \bigl(\mathcal{O}_X(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)\bigr),
  \]
  with restriction maps the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor of the three underlying
  restriction maps --- those of \(\mathcal{P}\), of the structure presheaf
  \(\mathcal{O}_X\) (the middle factor restricting along the ring restriction
  map), and of \(\mathcal{Q}\) --- is a functor
  \((\mathrm{Opens}\,X)^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{Ab}\). It is the \emph{domain}
  row of the relative-tensor coequalizer presentation
  (\cref{lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer}): objectwise it is exactly the
  triple tensor \(\mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X(U)
  \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)\) on which the two
  \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-action maps of
  \cref{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer} act, and its codomain row is
  the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaf \cref{def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}.
  Functoriality (the identity and composition laws of the restriction maps)
  follows from the functoriality of \(\mathrm{TensorProduct.map}\) --- the
  \(\mathrm{map\_id}\) and \(\mathrm{map\_comp}\) laws --- checked on elementary
  tensors by \(\otimes\)-induction.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[Left-action natural transformation of the coequalizer]
  \label{def:relTensorActL}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.relTensorActL}
  \uses{def:relTensorTriplePresheaf, def:relTensorDomainPresheaf,
    lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer}
  For presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) the
  \emph{left-action} natural transformation
  \[
    a_L : \bigl(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}
      (\mathcal{O}_X(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U))\bigr)
    \;\longrightarrow\;
    \bigl(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)\bigr)
  \]
  from the triple presheaf (\cref{def:relTensorTriplePresheaf}) to the
  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaf (\cref{def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}) is, on an
  open \(U\), the additive map
  \[
    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}
      \bigl(\mathcal{O}_X(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)\bigr)
    \longrightarrow
    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U),
    \qquad
    m \otimes (s \otimes n) \longmapsto (s \cdot m) \otimes n,
  \]
  collapsing the middle ring factor through the scalar action of
  \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\) on \(\mathcal{P}(U)\). This is exactly the objectwise
  action map \(\mathrm{actLmap}\) of
  \cref{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer}; together with the parallel
  right-action transformation it is one of the two rows whose coequalizer
  presents the relative tensor presheaf \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\)
  (\cref{lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer}). Naturality in \(U\) is the
  compatibility of the module action with the restriction maps: the restriction
  of \(s \cdot m\) equals \((s|_V) \cdot (m|_V)\), so \(a_L\) commutes with the
  triple-tensor and \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor restriction maps; checked on
  elementary tensors by \(\otimes\)-induction.
\end{definition}
